_note: Tom & Co original calculations and proprietary stats. Each Layer 2 article appends an entry. Each Layer 3 datapoint (survey, anonymised client outcome) is added manually by Tom with `layer: 3`. See .claude/skills/tomandco-article/stat-sourcing.md for the schema.

stats.stat: UK government direct AI funding announced or detailed across the twelve months covered by the AI Opportunities Action Plan: One Year On
applies_to: clusters: Strategy & ROI; Risk, Governance & Legal
stats.method: Recipe E (funding maths) — aggregation of named funding lines from a single primary government document

sources_used.title: AI Opportunities Action Plan: One Year On
sources_used.publisher: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT)

stats.workings: AISI 240 + AIRR 250 + Edinburgh supercomputer 750 + Sovereign AI Unit 500 + TechFirst 187 + Modern Industrial Strategy AI 150 + HDRS gov contribution 400 (of 600 total) + NDL 100 + Robotics/Made Smarter 40 + Smart Data 36 + AI Growth Zones direct 25 + AI Assurance Innovation Fund 11 + Regulators' Pioneer Fund 8.9 + TechLocal AI Accelerator 7.8 + AI Capability Fund 3.6 = 2,709.3 GBP million ≈ 2.6 GBP billion (rounded down conservatively). Excludes the £28.2bn private investment the AI Growth Zones programme is reported to have generated.

stats.stat: Annual turnover threshold below which EU AI Act percentage-of-turnover fines are always the operative (lower) cap rather than the fixed euro amount
stats.method: Recipe A — sterling conversion and break-even calculation of EU AI Act Article 99 penalty thresholds

sources_used.title: EU AI Act Article 99 — Penalties
sources_used.publisher: European Commission EU AI Act Service Desk

sources_used.title: EU AI Act full text (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689)
sources_used.publisher: Official Journal of the European Union

stats.workings: Tier 1 (prohibited AI): fixed cap €35m ÷ 7% = €500m break-even turnover. Tier 2 (high-risk and transparency): €15m ÷ 3% = €500m break-even. Tiers 1 and 2 share the same €500m break-even. EUR/GBP conversion at 0.845 (approximate June 2026 market rate): €500m × 0.845 = £422.5m, rounded conservatively to £420m. Below £420m annual global turnover, the % cap always produces the smaller fine in Tiers 1 and 2. Above £420m, the fixed euro cap is the binding limit. Virtually all UK SMEs and most UK mid-caps are below this threshold.

stats.stat: Break-even productivity saving for a 10-person UK knowledge-worker team on a business AI subscription (~£1,900/year)
stats.method: Recipe D — cost-per-outcome ratio using ONS median earnings data and published vendor pricing

sources_used.title: Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings (ASHE) 2024
ChatGPT Plans and Pricing

stats.workings: ONS ASHE 2024: median full-time weekly earnings £728 × 52 = £37,856/year gross. Employer NI (13.8% above £9,100 secondary threshold): (£37,856 − £9,100) × 0.138 = £3,968. Employer pension 3% minimum (on qualifying earnings above £6,240): (£37,856 − £6,240) × 0.03 = £948. Total employer cost: £37,856 + £3,968 + £948 = £42,772/year ≈ £42,800/year. Hourly rate at 1,800 productive hours/year: £42,800 / 1,800 = £23.78/hour. ChatGPT Business annual at ~£16/user/month: 10 × £16 × 12 = £1,920/year. Per-person annual cost: £192. Hours to break even per person: £192 / £23.78 = 8.07 hours/year. Minutes per week: (8.07 × 60) / 52 = 9.3 min/person/week ≈ under 10 minutes.

stats.stat: AI adoption velocity among the UK's largest solicitors' firms, 2020-2023
applies_to: clusters: UK Industry Verticals; Risk, Governance & Legal
stats.method: Recipe C — year-on-year delta derived from SRA Risk Outlook Report (November 2023) data

sources_used.title: SRA Risk Outlook report: The use of artificial intelligence in the legal market

stats.workings: SRA Risk Outlook (November 2023) states that 75% of the largest solicitors' firms were using AI, 'nearly twice the number from just three years ago.' Implied baseline (November 2020): 75% / ~2 = approximately 37.5%. Change over 3 years: 75% minus 37.5% = 37.5 percentage points. Annual adoption velocity: 37.5pp / 3 years = 12.5pp per year, rounded conservatively to 12pp. This calculation was completed before ChatGPT's mainstream availability (November 2022), so actual post-2022 velocity is likely higher.

stats.stat: Implied share of UK firms with 250 or more staff actually running agentic AI, mid-2025
applies_to: topic: agentic AI use cases for mid-market businesses
stats.method: Recipe C / cross-source derivation — combining two published UK adoption figures the sources did not multiply together

sources_used.title: Business insights and impact on the UK economy (Business Insights and Conditions Survey), bulletin 8 January 2026
AI Adoption Research (survey February to May 2025)
sources_used.publisher: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (IFF Research and Technopolis Group)

stats.workings: ONS BICS (late December 2025): 44% of UK firms with 250+ employees use some form of AI. DSIT AI Adoption Research (survey 12 Feb - 2 May 2025): agentic AI is the least-adopted AI technology at 7% of AI-using firms. Combined: 0.44 x 0.07 = 0.0308 = approximately 3.1% of large/mid-market UK firms running agentic AI in mid-2025. Conservative note: the two surveys use slightly different populations and reference dates, so treat as an order-of-magnitude estimate; neither source published the combined figure.

stats._note: Layer 2 calculation from this article, for the central process to merge into data/stat_bank.json. This subagent does NOT edit data/stat_bank.json directly.
stats.stat: Share of all UK businesses using generative/text-generation AI vs agentic AI, derived from DSIT adoption rates
stats.unit: percent of all UK businesses (approx. 12x gap)
applies_to: clusters: Technical & Build; Tools & emerging vocabulary; Strategy & ROI
stats.method: Recipe C — share-of-all-firms cut derived from a single primary snapshot (DSIT) that the source did not publish in this form

sources_used.title: AI Adoption Research
sources_used.publisher: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT)

stats.workings: DSIT (published 13 Feb 2026): 16% of all UK businesses use at least one AI technology; among adopters, 85% use text generation and 7% use agentic AI. Share of ALL UK businesses using text generation = 0.16 x 0.85 = 0.136 = 13.6%. Share using agentic AI = 0.16 x 0.07 = 0.0112 = ~1.1%. Ratio = 85/7 = ~12.1x (holds across all firms as the 16% base cancels). Result: generative/text-generation AI reaches ~13.6% of all UK businesses vs ~1.1% for agentic AI, a ~12x gap.

stats._note: Proposed Layer 2 entry for data/stat_bank.json. Written to the article output folder only; the central process merges it. This subagent does NOT edit data/stat_bank.json directly (per ad-hoc batch instructions).
stats.stat: Orchestration cost premium of a multi-agent orchestrated AI system over a single AI agent
applies_to: clusters: Technical & Build; Tools & emerging vocabulary
stats.method: Recipe D — cost-per-outcome ratio derived from published Anthropic token multipliers

sources_used.title: How we built our multi-agent research system

stats.workings: Anthropic reports a multi-agent system uses about 15x the tokens of a chat, and a single agent uses about 4x the tokens of a chat. Dividing isolates the orchestration premium: 15 / 4 = 3.75x. Illustrative sterling example: a request costing a single agent £100 in model spend runs to roughly £375 for an orchestrated multi-agent version. Caveat: token count is a proxy for model spend and assumes comparable per-token pricing; it is a coordination-cost indicator, not full TCO (excludes engineering, latency, observability).

stats.stat: Estimated sterling saving per employee per year from applying McKinsey's reported 10-20% AI cost reduction (software engineering, IT and manufacturing functions) to the fully loaded cost of a median UK employee
stats.method: Recipe A — sterling conversion applying a published cost-reduction percentage to ONS UK median employee cost data

sources_used.title: Employee earnings in the UK: 2025 (ASHE 2025 provisional results)
Rates and thresholds for employers 2026 to 2027
The state of AI: How organizations are rewiring to capture value

stats.workings: ONS ASHE 2025: median gross annual earnings for full-time employees in post at least a year, April 2025 = GBP 39,039. Employer NI 2026/27: 15% on earnings above the GBP 5,000 secondary threshold = (39,039 - 5,000) x 0.15 = GBP 5,105.85. Employer minimum pension (3% on qualifying earnings band GBP 6,240-GBP 50,270, unchanged for 2026/27) = (39,039 - 6,240) x 0.03 = GBP 983.97. Fully loaded employer cost = 39,039 + 5,105.85 + 983.97 = GBP 45,128.82, rounded to GBP 45,100. Applying McKinsey's reported 10-20% cost reduction in software engineering, IT and manufacturing functions: low end 45,100 x 0.10 = GBP 4,510; high end 45,100 x 0.20 = GBP 9,020. Rounded to a GBP 4,500-GBP 9,000 per employee per year range.

stats.stat: Sterling value of a Made Smarter-backed UK manufacturer's reported AI invoice-processing time saving, at ONS median admin/secretarial pay
stats.method: Recipe A (sterling conversion) — converting a reported hours-saved case study into a loaded sterling value using ONS pay data

sources_used.title: First wave of South East manufacturers geared for growth with Made Smarter
Employee earnings in the UK: 2025 (ASHE 2025)
Rates and thresholds for employers 2026 to 2027

stats.workings: Made Smarter case study: Nordell's AI-powered invoice processing is expected to save more than 60 hours of administration every month, i.e. 720 hours/year. ONS ASHE 2025: full-time median hourly pay for administrative and secretarial occupations = GBP 14.80. Grossed to an annual full-time-equivalent salary at 37.5 hours/week x 52 weeks: 14.80 x 37.5 x 52 = GBP 28,860. Employer NI 2026/27 (15% above the GBP 5,000 secondary threshold): (28,860 - 5,000) x 0.15 = GBP 3,579. Employer minimum auto-enrolment pension (3% on qualifying earnings above GBP 6,240): (28,860 - 6,240) x 0.03 = GBP 678.60. Fully loaded annual cost: 28,860 + 3,579 + 678.60 = GBP 33,117.60. Loaded hourly rate at 1,950 hours/year (37.5 x 52): 33,117.60 / 1,950 = GBP 16.98/hour. Value of 720 hours/year saved: 720 x 16.98 = GBP 12,225.60, rounded to approximately GBP 12,200/year. This values only the reclaimed staff time; it excludes the AI software's own subscription cost and any Made Smarter grant contribution, so it is a gross saving figure, not a net ROI.

stats.stat: Blended annual AI tooling budget range for a 10-seat UK team across five commonly cited subscription tiers
stats.method: Recipe D (cost-per-outcome ratio across vendors) — blending several vendors' published per-seat pricing into a single team-level annual budget range that no single vendor publishes

sources_used.title: GitHub Copilot Plans and pricing
ChatGPT Business pricing
Claude pricing
Microsoft 365 Copilot pricing

stats.workings: Per-seat monthly list prices: GitHub Copilot Business $19, ChatGPT Business $20 (annual billing), Claude Team Standard $20 (annual billing), Microsoft 365 Copilot £30 (add-on), Claude Team Premium with Claude Code $100 (annual billing). Dollar prices converted at $1 = £0.75 (July 2026): $19 = £14.25, $20 = £15, $100 = £75. Each annualised for a 10-seat team (price x 10 x 12): GitHub Copilot Business £1,710; ChatGPT Business £1,800; Claude Team Standard £1,800; Microsoft 365 Copilot £3,600; Claude Team Premium £9,000. Range across all five tiers: £1,710 (lowest) to £9,000 (highest).

stats.stat: UK businesses using AI in some form but not yet integrated into their own systems (the 'shallow adoption' gap)
stats.method: Recipe B (sector/segment cut) — combining two figures from a single primary survey with a UK business population count the survey did not itself apply

sources_used.title: Future of Work: AI in the Workplace (press release: 'Half of SMEs using AI, with limited headcount impact so far')
sources_used.publisher: British Chambers of Commerce and University of Essex ESRC Centre for Micro-Social Change (MiSoC)

sources_used.title: Business population estimates for the UK and regions 2024: statistical release

stats.workings: BCC/Essex (March 2026): 54% of UK SMEs use AI in some form; ~11% have moved beyond generic tools (ChatGPT, Copilot) into deeper, bespoke integration with their own systems. Gap = 54% - 11% = 43 percentage points using AI without deep integration. Applied to gov.uk's 5,499,000 UK private-sector businesses (start of 2024): 0.43 x 5,499,000 = 2,364,570, rounded to approximately 2.4 million. Caveat: the BCC/Essex sample is SMEs specifically, while the gov.uk figure covers all UK private-sector businesses; since SMEs are the large majority of that population by count, the two are treated as broadly comparable, but this is an order-of-magnitude estimate, not an exact calculation. Neither source publishes the combined figure.

stats.stat: UK businesses now using AI for marketing or content work
stats.method: Recipe B (combination cut) — combining BCC/Essex SME AI adoption and marketing-use-case share with gov.uk's UK business population estimate

sources_used.title: Half of SMEs using AI, with limited headcount impact so far (BCC and University of Essex, with Atos, March 2026)
Business population estimates for the UK and regions 2024: statistical release

stats.workings: BCC/Essex/Atos March 2026 survey: 54% of UK SMEs use AI in some form; of AI-using SMEs, marketing is a joint-top use case at 72% (tied with admin). 0.54 x 0.72 = 0.3888, i.e. 38.88% of the UK private-sector business population. Applied to gov.uk's 5,499,000 UK private-sector businesses (2024 estimate): 0.3888 x 5,499,000 = 2,138,011, rounded to 'roughly 2.1 million'.
